title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two friends discussing a new fitness trend called 'Primal Movement'.
extracts.audio_script: Man: So, I saw you crawling across the park like a lizard this morning. Taking up 'Primal Movement', are we? Woman: Don't knock it until you've tried it! [1]It’s actually much more cognitively demanding than just mindlessly pounding the treadmill[/1]. You have to be so aware of your coordination. Man: I don't doubt it, but it looks a bit... well, eccentric for a public space. I think I’ll stick to my weightlifting. Woman: That’s the thing, though. Weightlifting is so linear. This is all about functional mobility. [2]I’ve noticed a massive difference in my lower back pain since I started[/2], which years of traditional gym work never touched. Man: Fair enough. I suppose if it works, the social embarrassment is a small price to pay. Woman: Exactly. Besides, once you get into the flow, you stop caring about who’s watching anyway.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: So, I saw you crawling across the park like a lizard this morning. Taking up 'Primal Movement', are we?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Don't knock it until you've tried it! It’s actually much more cognitively demanding than just mindlessly pounding the treadmill. You have to be so aware of your coordination.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I don't doubt it, but it looks a bit... well, eccentric for a public space. I think I’ll stick to my weightlifting.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: That’s the thing, though. Weightlifting is so linear. This is all about functional mobility. I’ve noticed a massive difference in my lower back pain since I started, which years of traditional gym work never touched.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Fair enough. I suppose if it works, the social embarrassment is a small price to pay.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Exactly. Besides, once you get into the flow, you stop caring about who’s watching anyway.

questions.question: What does the woman find particularly beneficial about this type of exercise?

options.A: It is less physically exhausting than running.
options.B: It requires a high level of mental engagement.
options.C: It is easier to perform in a social setting.

questions.explanation: She describes it as 'cognitively demanding', implying that it challenges the brain/mind more than traditional cardio.

questions.question: The woman mentions her back pain to demonstrate that the exercise...

options.A: is safer than lifting heavy weights.
options.B: is more effective than her previous routines.
options.C: should only be done under professional supervision.

questions.explanation: She notes a 'massive difference' that 'traditional gym work never touched', showing its superior effectiveness for her condition.

extracts.context: You hear two professionals, a man and a woman, discussing the use of AI in their industry.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I’m starting to think our latest software update is more trouble than it’s worth. The AI suggestions for the layouts are just... bizarre. Woman: Really? [3]I’ve found it quite liberating to have a starting point that isn't my own biased habit[/3]. It throws up combinations I’d never have considered. Man: But it lacks any sense of brand identity. It’s technically proficient but emotionally hollow. Woman: Well, [4]it’s not meant to replace us, but to act as a springboard[/4]. You still have to apply the finishing touches. Man: I worry that clients will start to wonder why they’re paying our fees if a machine is doing the heavy lifting. Woman: On the contrary, it allows us to spend more time on the high-level strategy rather than the grunt work. It’s about shifting our value proposition. Man: I hope you're right, but I can't help feeling a bit redundant lately.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’m starting to think our latest software update is more trouble than it’s worth. The AI suggestions for the layouts are just... bizarre.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Really? I’ve found it quite liberating to have a starting point that isn't my own biased habit. It throws up combinations I’d never have considered.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: But it lacks any sense of brand identity. It’s technically proficient but emotionally hollow.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Well, it’s not meant to replace us, but to act as a springboard. You still have to apply the finishing touches.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I worry that clients will start to wonder why they’re paying our fees if a machine is doing the heavy lifting.

dialogue.speaker: woman

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I hope you're right, but I can't help feeling a bit redundant lately.

questions.question: What does the woman appreciate about the new software?

options.A: It matches the brand identity perfectly.
options.B: It helps her overcome her own creative patterns.
options.C: It is much faster than previous versions.

questions.explanation: She mentions it is 'liberating' because the starting point isn't her 'own biased habit', allowing for new combinations.

questions.question: In the woman's view, the role of AI in their work is to...

options.A: take over the strategic planning of projects.
options.B: provide an initial basis for further development.
options.C: increase the fees they can charge to clients.

questions.explanation: She uses the metaphor of a 'springboard', meaning something that provides a start for further action.

extracts.context: You hear two friends from Ireland, Emily and Connor, discussing a recent documentary about urban planning.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: Did you see that documentary last night about '15-minute cities'? Man: I did. It’s a compelling concept, but [5]I think they glossed over the logistical nightmare of retrofitting older European cities[/5]. Woman: I thought the same. It’s all well and good for new developments, but how do you move a hospital or a major industrial zone? Man: Exactly. However, [6]the section on reclaiming street space for pedestrians was quite eye-opening[/6]. The transformation of those plazas in Paris was incredible. Woman: It was, though I wonder if it would go down as well here. People are very attached to their cars, aren't they? Man: True, there'd be an uproar if they tried to pedestrianise the main thoroughfare tomorrow. But the documentary made a strong case for the long-term health benefits. Woman: It did. It’s just getting people to see past the immediate inconvenience that’s the hard part.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Did you see that documentary last night about '15-minute cities'?

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I did. It’s a compelling concept, but I think they glossed over the logistical nightmare of retrofitting older European cities.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I thought the same. It’s all well and good for new developments, but how do you move a hospital or a major industrial zone?

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Exactly. However, the section on reclaiming street space for pedestrians was quite eye-opening. The transformation of those plazas in Paris was incredible.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It was, though I wonder if it would go down as well here. People are very attached to their cars, aren't they?

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: True, there'd be an uproar if they tried to pedestrianise the main thoroughfare tomorrow. But the documentary made a strong case for the long-term health benefits.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It did. It’s just getting people to see past the immediate inconvenience that’s the hard part.

questions.question: What was the man's main criticism of the documentary?

options.A: It focused too much on historical architecture.
options.B: It underestimated the difficulty of adapting existing infrastructure.
options.C: It failed to provide examples from outside Europe.

questions.explanation: He says they 'glossed over the logistical nightmare of retrofitting', which means to treat a complex problem as if it were simple.

questions.question: Which aspect of the documentary did the man find particularly impressive?

options.A: The health statistics provided by experts.
options.B: The interviews with Parisian city officials.
options.C: The visual evidence of successful urban changes.

questions.explanation: He describes the section on 'reclaiming street space' and the 'transformation' of Paris as 'eye-opening'.
